articles.title: Small Firm, Big Compensation — See Also
articles.content:
**AZA涨薪了**： Ahmad, Zavitsanos & Mensing P.C.律所因卓越表现而给予合伙人丰厚报酬！

**一分价钱一分货**：特朗普的律师们不断错过简单的提交截止日期——这对他们来说怎么这么难？

**被解雇的法学院院长提起性歧视诉讼**：尽管成绩斐然，他在与丈夫结婚几个月后被解雇。

**目前只是提名阶段**：Todd Blanche能否真正获得确认？

**“死律师”理论**：大型语言模型和惰性会取代真正的人工法律服务吗？

本文最初发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
AZA Bumps Up Their Pay Scale: Ahmad, Zavitsanos & Mensing P.C. pay their associates for their excellence!

You Get What You Pay For: Trump’s lawyers keep missing easy deadlines.

Fired Law School Dean Files Sexual Discrimination Suit: Despite clear successes, he was fired several months after marrying his husband.

Just A Nomination For Now: Will Todd Blanche actually get confirmed?

Dead Lawyer Theory: Will LLMs and laziness push out actual human lawyering?

The post Small Firm, Big Compensation — See Also appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Biglaw Partner Headed For The Federal Judiciary
articles.content:
编辑注：欢迎来到我们的每日专栏“每日趣味问答”！

参议员约翰·费特曼（John Fetterman）退还了安东尼奥·M·波佐斯（Antonio M. Pozos）担任宾夕法尼亚州东区法官提名的“蓝卡”，使该提名得以推进。请问波佐斯是哪家大型律所的合伙人？

提示：这位密歇根法学院校友在进入大型律所之前，曾在司法部担任联邦检察官。

答案见下页。

本文《即将出任联邦司法机构的大型律所合伙人》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. Note: Welcome to our daily feature Trivia Question of the Day!

Senator John Fetterman returned the “blue slip” for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania nomination of Antonio M. Pozos, allowing the nom to move forward. Which Biglaw firm counts Pozos among its partners?

Hint: The Michigan Law grad was a federal prosecutor at the Department of Justice before he moved to Biglaw.

See the answer on the next page.

The post Biglaw Partner Headed For The Federal Judiciary appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Meeting Clients Where They Are In An AI World
articles.content:
当你的客户比你更懂法律和策略时，你会怎么做？或者至少他们认为自己更懂。这就是如今许多律师和法律专业人士面临的困境。客户走进办公室时，带着从公开AI系统获取的各种建议和信息，而这些信息往往都是错误的。

**让我们面对现实吧，客户正在使用AI**

最近我和几位刑事律师讨论了这个话题。他们说这种情况经常发生。问题不仅仅是客户掌握的信息可能正确也可能不正确，甚至可能毫无帮助，而是客户对这些信息的迷之自信。当信息或建议的策略错误时，这使得律师的工作更加困难。律师需要花费大量时间来反复说服客户，那个机器人的建议是不靠谱的。当然，那个机器人总是以极其自信的语气说出各种观点。

我相信民事领域的情况也是一样的。问题不仅在于说服客户相信他们从机器人那里得到的信息是糟糕的，更在于这正在播下不信任的种子。法律和策略往往是灰色地带。很多时候，并没有一个明确的正确答案，也没有万无一失的策略或保证的结果。最佳答案往往源于律师的经验、判断和专业知识，而这些是机器人无法复制的。

**但如果我有一个更好的律师**

当客户开始根据机器人告诉他们的话怀疑律师的建议时，他们开始认为：如果我有一个更好的律师，我会得到更好的结果——一个会按照ChatGPT建议去做的律师。再加上AI工具经常告诉用户它认为用户想听的话，这就形成了一个真正令人困扰的问题。这个问题会导致大量的时间和精力浪费在试图说服客户应该听你的而不是ChatGPT的。

而且这不仅仅是在案件开始时客户走进门那一刻的问题。这种情况贯穿整个案件。如果客户在见律师之前先去咨询ChatGPT，你可以打赌他们会在律师告诉他们不想听或不相信的事情时再次回到ChatGPT。这将导致越来越多与人类律师的争论、不信任和干扰。

更不用说客户可能在与机器人的讨论中泄露律师-客户特权信息。正如我之前讨论过的，将机密材料放入公开AI平台会冒着丧失特权保护的风险，因为它可能不再被视为机密。律师自己使用AI时尚且难以避免这种放弃。对于一个试图获得他们想听的答案的客户来说，不向机器人透露超出必要的信息几乎是不可能的。

**使用AI来扭转局面**

然而，与我交谈的一位刑事律师确实有一个解决方案。当客户告诉他某个建议来自机器人且是错误的时候，他会询问客户提供了什么输入信息。然后他试图向他们展示，通过正确的输入、提出正确的问题，可以获得更准确的结果，而且通常——他说——与他的建议相符。他还会向客户展示，通过调整提示词来引导期望的结果会如何影响输出。

当然，成功做到这一点需要时间、努力和理解AI工作原理及如何构建有效提示的能力。但这比与客户持续争论AI输出要好得多。而且坦率地说，这也需要一些勇气，因为存在机器人可能是对的而律师是错的可能性。这是一个令人不舒服的现实。

但如果真是这样，那就更好了。通过构建好的提示词，AI有时确实能产生好的建议，我们应该对不同的观点保持开放态度。坦率地说，律师应该在事前就使用AI工具来检验自己的观点和想法，以便为客户带着AI生成的信息来访做好准备。

我喜欢这个想法。它节省时间，减少干扰，建立信任。我发现，多年来，如果你能预见客户的想法和说法，然后向他们展示为什么他们的想法是错误的，这会增强他们对你的信心。以教育的方式使用AI，使关系更像是一个团队——律师、客户和AI工具一起合作，以更少的摩擦获得更好的结果。

而且，这与我们职业道德规则1.1的能力要求相符，该规则要求我们了解并理解技术的优势和风险。这不仅适用于我们作为律师使用的技术，也适用于我们可以合理预期客户会使用的技术。而今天，那就是AI。

我们的客户，像几乎所有人一样，将会就他们的法律问题和我们告诉他们的事情咨询AI。所以我们也最好使用AI。不使用客户正在使用的工具可能导致非常紧张和不令人满意的律师-客户关系。执业法律已经够难了，让我们不要让它变得更难。

所以让我们面对现实，在AI时代与客户建立联系。当客户走进你的办公室时，准备好你的机器人。

斯蒂芬·恩布里是一名律师、演讲者、博主和作家。他出版《科技法律交叉路口》，这是一个致力于审视技术、法律和执业法律之间紧张关系的博客。

这篇文章《在AI世界中与客户建立联系》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
What do you do when your client knows more about the law and strategy than you do? Or at least thinks they do. That’s the dilemma a lot of lawyers and legal professionals face these days. Clients come into the office armed with all sorts of advice and information they have obtained from publicly facing AI systems, information which is often just wrong. 

Let’s Face It, Clients Are Using AI

I was talking to a couple of criminal lawyers recently about this very subject. They say it happens all the time. And it’s not just that the clients have information that may or may not be correct or even helpful. It’s the confidence that the clients seem to have in that information. Which makes the lawyer’s job that much harder when the information or recommended strategy is wrong. It takes time away from the case handling to constantly try to convince the client that the bot is off base. A bot that, of course, says things with such confidence.

I’m sure it’s the same on the civil side. The problem is not just one of convincing the client that what they got from the bot is bad, it’s the seeds of mistrust that are being sown. The law and strategy are often gray areas. Many times, there is no clearly right answer, no foolproof strategy or guaranteed outcome. The best answers often stem from the lawyers’ combined experience, judgment and know how, much of which can’t be duplicated by a bot.

If I Had A Better Lawyer

But when the client begins to wonder that the lawyer’s recommendations are wrong based on what a bot tells them, the client starts to think: I would be getting a better result if I had a better lawyer. A lawyer who would do what ChatGPT recommends.  

Add to this the fact that all too often AI tools will tell a person what it thinks the person wants to hear. And you have real and nagging problem. It’s a problem that will lead to a lot of time and energy being spent trying to convince the client that they should be listeing to you, not ChatGPT.

And it’s a problem not just at the beginning of the case when the client walks in the door. It runs throughout the case. If the client goes to ChatGPT before they see their lawyer, you can bet that they are going to go back to ChatGPT every time the lawyer tells them something they don’t want to hear or don’t believe. Which will lead to more and more arguments, distrust, and disruption for the human lawyer. 

Not to mention the fact that the client may be revealing attorney-client privileged information in its discussions with the bot. As I have previously discussed, placing confidential material in a public AI platform risks waiving the privilege since it may no longer be considered confidential. It’s hard enough for lawyers to use AI and avoid this waiver. It may be well-nigh impossible for a client trying to get the answer they want to hear to not tell the bot more than they should.

Using AI To Turn The Tables

One of the criminal lawyers I was talking to did have a bit of a solution, however. His strategy when a client tells him something that came from a bot that’s wrong is to ask the client about the input they provided. He then tries to show them that with a proper input, asking the right question, a more accurate result can be obtained that’s often, he says, in line with his advice. He also shows the client how by working with the prompt to suggest a desired result affects the outcome. 

Of course, successfully doing what this lawyer suggests takes time, work and effort. It also requires a good understanding of how AI works and how to construct prompts. But it’s better than having an ongoing battle with the client over AI outputs. And, frankly, it also takes some courage since there’s a risk that the bot may be right and the lawyer wrong. That’s a reality that is uncomfortable.

But if so, so much the better. By constructing good prompts, AI sometimes comes up with good stuff and we owe it to our clients to be open to different views. Frankly, the lawyer should be checking their views and ideas against AI tools on the front end to be prepared for the client armed with AI-generated information.

I like the idea. It saves time. It reduces disruption. It breeds trust. I found over the years, for example, that if you can anticipate what a client thinks and will say and then show them why they are misguided, it enhances their confidence in you. And using AI in an educational way moves the relationship more to one of a team with a lawyer, client, and AI tools all working together to get better results with less friction. 

And by the way, it squares with our ethical duty under the competency Rule 1.1 to know and understand the benefits and risks of technology. That applies not only to the technology we use as lawyers but the technology we can reasonably expect our clients will be using. And today, that’s AI. 

Our clients, like virtually everyone else, are going to be consulting AI about their legal problems and what we are telling them. So we better be using AI as well. Not using tools our clients are using can lead to a very tense and unsatisfying attorney client relationship. Practicing law is hard enough, let’s not make it harder.

So let’s get real and meet our clients where they are. When the client walks into your office, be ready with your bot.

Stephen Embry is a lawyer, speaker, blogger, and writer. He publishes TechLaw Crossroads, a blog devoted to the examination of the tension between technology, the law, and the practice of law.

The post Meeting Clients Where They Are In An AI World appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: The Emersonian Lawyer: The Compass And The Resume
articles.content:
按语：这是“埃默森式律师”系列文章的第一篇。该系列探讨拉尔夫·瓦尔多·爱默生的哲学对于法律职业中特定压力、妥协和可能性的启示。系列文章援引爱默生的核心散文《自助论》《补偿论》《圆》《超灵》《诗人》和《自然》，并将其应用于法律实务的具体运作机制。这不是一套自助类读物，而是一套哲学读物，以从业者的视角撰写。

有一个问题，大多数律师从未问过，不是因为它无解，而是因为资格证书的机器从未停歇足够长的时间，让他们得以追问。你是在追随自己的指南针，还是在追随简历？这两者并非同一个方向。它们甚至可能指向不同的半球。

简历遵循那些清晰可见的东西：看起来正确的书记员职位、名字正确的律所、正在扩张的业务组。指南针遵循的是更难命名的东西：当你以某种特定方式处理某种特定类型的问题时，你感到自己正在做自己应该做的事。不是表演。不是执行。是存在。大多数律师都能回忆起上一次有这种感觉的时刻。那段记忆与今天之间相隔的年数，值得留意。

场景

这是一个星期二的傍晚。一位大型律所的合伙人正在审阅一位初级律师的备忘录。工作很出色：详尽、技术扎实、结构无可挑剔。合伙人像往常一样修改：收紧分析、软化结论、调整语调，让客户听到他们需要听到的内容，而非法律实际所说的。她非常擅长这些。她已经这样做了十一年。她把备忘录发回去，上面有修订痕迹。她合上笔记本电脑。她在安静的办公室里坐了一会儿。这一刻过去了。她又打开笔记本电脑。还有十四封邮件等着处理。

这个场景不是悲剧。它甚至算不上异常。从一切外在标准来看，这位律师是成功的。她是一家大所的合伙人，这意味着她在一场残酷淘汰的选拔过程中存活了下来。她的收入超过了她父母一辈子的总和。从资格证书机器的账本来看，她是明确的成功者。账本没有记录的是，在那安静的一刻，她感受到又未能完全感受到的东西。

机器

法律行业建立了任何专业领域中最精密的资格证书体系之一。法学院排名，根据LSAT分数和本科GPA将申请者分类为数字等级。《法律评论》会员资格，由赢得去年比赛的在校生组织的竞赛授予。联邦书记员职位，其授予的声望与法院距华盛顿的距离成反比。横向市场信号，以业务量的基点传达你的价值。合伙人晋升通道，再次筛选，反复筛选，直到幸存者以头衔和股权出现在终点，有时在那一刻才发现，他们一直在追求的东西并非想象的那样。

这些资格证书每一个都是真实的。每一个都传达了持有者身上某些真实的东西。LSAT测量了某些东西。《法律评论》会员资格展示了某些东西。联邦书记员职位确实是塑造性的。这些都不是虚构。但资格证书机器所传达的，作为一个系统，并非你是谁。它是你对那些需要快速分类人的机构的可辨识程度。这是两码事，混淆它们的后果缓慢累积、骤然到来。

机器并非恶意的。它不是阴谋。它是一种分类机制，做着分类机制该做的事：优化它能测量的特征，让它无法测量的特征变得不可见。机器可以测量你的LSAT分数。它无法测量合同诉讼是否是你生来要解决的问题。它可以测量你的法学院GPA。它无法测量你服务的客户是否是你愿意将自己的岁月倾注其问题的人。

分类功能是有用的。它只是不是指南针。

爱默生看到了什么

拉尔夫·瓦尔多·爱默生不是在写律师。他是在写诗人。但他在1844年一篇名为《诗人》的文章中所观察到的，适用于每一个选择了资格证书而非天职的专业人士，或者那些在职业生涯中途发现可能不自觉地这样做了的人。

爱默生区分了两类人：被环境塑造的人（成为机构需要他们成为的人）和被某种并非完全自主选择的天职所驱动的人。他论证，第一类人变得可辨识。第二类人变得在场。这不是同一成就。

他在《自助论》中的论证更进一步。爱默生暗示，以资格证书为先的人是在坦白什么：他们信任机构对他们价值的判断胜过信任自己的判断。他们已将“我是谁”这个问题外包给了有权认证的机构。这不是虚荣或软弱。这是对一个奖励可辨识性、惩罚模糊性的系统的理性回应。但爱默生坚持，这也是一种自我放弃，渐进得让大多数人从未注意到它何时发生。

简历回答一个问题：做了什么机构会认可的事？指南针回答一个不同的问题：你来这里要做什么？这两个问题在幸运的情况下可能产生相同的答案。但资格证书机器并非为产生那种幸运而设计。它是为生产律师而设计的。可辨识的、有资格证书的、被机构分类的律师，而非这个特定的律师，有着这特定的本质和这特定的问题——而这正是他们生来要解决的。机器不知道你是谁。它只知道你考了多少分。

但机器遗漏了某些先于分数的东西，爱默生在他第一本书中命名了它。那本书出版于1836年，比《诗人》早八年，比《自助论》找到其著名的表述形式也早八年。《自然》是爱默生的基础论断：存在一个先于机构建构的现实。资格证书机器没有创造它。排名没有测量它。书记员职位没有授予它。它是什么：一个人实际的本性，他们特定的专注品质，真正吸引他们的问题，激活他们最深潜流的工作。爱默生称之为一个人的nature，他用的是这个词的本义。不是气质。不是人格。你是什么和你为什么的实际根基，存在于机器在其上层层叠加的一切之下。

在《自然》中，爱默生描述了他称为“透明的眼球”的时刻：真正的感知体验，当自我退后，剩下的就是与现实原貌的直接接触。机器迫使律师服务于自我和机构。指南针做的是相反的事：剥去自我，让律师服务于法律本身。这更接近于见树又见林，而非任何心流状态；更接近于把握整体，而非任何舒适的契合感。去过那里的律师知道。不是因为它令人舒适。是因为它真实。

换言之，指南针不是一种偏好。它是对一直存在的东西的回应。机器没有创造它。它只能遮蔽它。

指南针是粗糙的仪器。它不告诉你目的地。但它足以让古代航海者离开熟悉的海岸线，发现世界。

没用机器的人

小奥里斯·哈廷顿·杰克逊生于1892年，宾夕法尼亚州斯普林克里克。他上了奥尔巴尼法学院一年，然后离开了。他在纽约州詹姆斯敦跟随一位名叫弗兰克·莫特的乡村律师学徒，在莫特的办公室里读案例，陪他上法庭，通过实践学习法律实务。1913年，他未经法律学位即获准执业。按照资格证书机器的逻辑，他并不完全可辨识。他后来成为美国副总检察长，然后是司法部长，然后是最高法院大法官。1945年，哈里·杜鲁门任命他为纽伦堡审判的首席美国检察官，在那里，杰克逊不得不为审判没有先例的罪行构建法律架构：发明类别、起草起诉书、在一个从未存在过的法庭上为从未被起诉过的行为提出辩护。

杰克逊完成这些，不是因为他有正确的资格证书。他完成这些，是因为他整个职业生涯都追随一个指南针，始终指向一个问题：法律与政治权力的关系是什么，当这种关系破裂时对文明意味着什么？那个问题引领他从纽约州北部的乡村律师事务所，经由新政，到最高法院，到巴伐利亚的一间法庭——在那里，它比任何资格证书所能准备的都更重要。

根据他自己的说法和所有认识他的人的描述，他也是一位埃默森式的人。他深入阅读爱默生，一生不断回归。他以在爱默生散文中发现的原则为榜样来规范自己的职业行为——坚持独立判断、抵制被任何单一客户或机构俘获、致力于阐述法律而非仅仅赢得官司。

杰克逊在此出现，不是作为效仿的榜样。很少有律师会构建国际法庭。他作为更有用的东西出现：证明指南针路线存在。证明追随你实际的本质而非资格证书序列并非天真。证明历史偶尔需要的律师，不是机器最可辨识的那些。

他也是一种挑衅。杰克森利用面前的东西：詹姆斯敦可用的案件、走进莫特门的客户、新政期间开放的政府职位。他没有等待正确的资格证书到来才开始。他以指南针定位，然后行动。

关于杰克逊，还有一件事需要诚实地说。他在此不是作为一个完美无缺的人物提供的。他在种族问题上的记录（包括他在法律裁决中的角色，现在被视为该职业最具影响力的失败案例之一）属于对他是谁和他做了什么完整记述的一部分。他追随的方向对本论证至关重要。而那个方向并非他独有。

如果指南针指向一个先于机构建构的现实（指向一种在任何排名系统分类之前就已存在的本性），那么它对机器所排斥的每个人都是可及的，正如它对杰克逊可及一样。机器在历史上否认了不像他那样的律师的指南针路线：通过律师资格考试的壁垒、通过依赖个人关系的书记员网络、通过整个资格证书体系的架构——而这个系列正是要审视这种架构。埃默森式论证是对那种否认的批判，而非对某一历史时刻中绕过它的那一个人的庆祝。方向是普遍的。机器是使这种接近不平等地分配的。这是控诉的一部分。

这个系列是什么

“埃默森式律师”是一系列关于拉尔夫·瓦尔多·爱默生哲学对执业律师、法学院学生和已经开始怀疑资格证书机器与指南针正指向不同方向的任何法律专业人士的启示的文章。它不是健康系列。它不会为你提供平衡，或弹性策略，或关于如何减少计时工作量的建议。



法律行业已有相关书籍，它们并非错误，只是远远不够。它们治疗症状，却未触及诊断本身。诊断可以直白地表述为：法律行业构建了一套系统，持续数十年来大规模生产着技术胜任却与职业使命疏离的律师。美国律师协会的自身数据（显示焦虑、抑郁和职业不满的比例持续处于所有需要资质的职业中最高之列）并非异常现象，而是这个系统的产出。爱默生对此有所论述。并非因为他曾写过关于律师的内容，而是因为他恰好写的就是这件事：当一个人的职业生涯建立在制度可辨识性而非真正使命感之上时会发生什么，以及如何循着指南针的方向重新导航。这个系列源自与法学哲学家凯文·李教授的持续对话，他在公民人格主义方面的研究既对爱默生式框架构成挑战，也提供了有益补充。他的挑战——这个系列将直接回应的问题是：指南针路线是否指向真正具有普世性的方向，还是历史上只有那些本已具备地位、符合杰克逊形象、在机器为之服务的网络中游走的律师才能获得？这一挑战使论证更加锋利。这个系列给出的回答是：指南针指向的是制度筛选之前的东西，对被机器排斥的所有人同等开放。机器才是限制准入的根源。指出这种限制既是爱默生所做之事，也是这个系列要做的事。

这个系列将深入探讨爱默生的五篇核心文章（《自立》《补偿》《圆》《诗人》《超灵》）以及作为它们根基的基础文本《自然》。每篇文章将其中一篇文本应用于法律职业生活中的一个具体摩擦点。沿此脉络，杰克逊将作为系列的传记主人公：不是圣人，不是简单化的英雄，而是一位在真实的制度条件下实践了爱默生式做法、留下了如何做到这一点的记录、并诚实审视自己不足之处的律师。

后续文章将具体而微。它们将把资质机器的各个部件逐一命名。它们将把爱默生哲学应用于计费小时制度、横向市场、合伙人晋升通道、法务角色、被束缚的判断力——即那些因机构要求而软化备忘录的律师的判断力。它们不会告诉你应该怎么做。爱默生不会告诉你应该怎么做。他所做的（这个系列将尝试去做的）是赋予你词汇，去表达那些你可能已有感知却未能找到语言来命名的东西。指南针始终存在。机器的声音非常嘈杂。但指南针始终存在。

杰夫·考克斯是Steno公司的品牌总监，同时是一名佛罗里达州执业律师。他的职业生涯横跨花旗集团的法律运营、UniCourt的法律数据与分析、欧华律师事务所的思想领导力战略，以及vLex的人工智能产品营销。作为一位多产的作者，他在Above the Law、Legaltech News、美国律师协会期刊及其他主要出版物上发表了一百多篇文章。他曾担任2025年海湾地区法律服务社董事会主席——这是坦帕湾地区一家为低收入居民提供免费民事法律服务的非营利性律师事务所——目前担任其发展委员会和人工智能工作组成员。
articles.summary:
Auth. note: This is the first essay in The Emersonian Lawyer, a series about what Ralph Waldo Emerson’s philosophy has to say about the specific pressures, compromises, and possibilities of a legal career. The series draws on Emerson’s core essays, Self-Reliance, Compensation, Circles, The Over-Soul, The Poet, and Nature, and applies them to the actual mechanics of practicing law. It is not a self-help series. It is a philosophical one, written in a practitioner’s register.

There is a question most lawyers never ask, not because it is unanswerable, but because the credential machine never stops long enough to let them.

Are you following your compass, or your résumé?

These are not the same direction. They may not even point toward the same hemisphere. The résumé follows what is legible: the clerkship that looks right, the firm with the correct name, the practice group that is growing. The compass follows something harder to name: the sense, when you are doing a particular kind of work with a particular kind of problem, that you are doing exactly what you are supposed to be doing. Not performing. Not executing. Being.

Most lawyers can remember the last time they felt that. The number of years between that memory and today is worth noting.

The Scene

It is late on a Tuesday. A partner at a large firm is reviewing a junior associate’s memo. The work is excellent: thorough, technically sound, impeccably structured. The partner edits it the way she always edits it: tightening the analysis, softening the conclusion, adjusting the register so the client hears what it needs to hear rather than what the law actually says. She is very good at this. She has been very good at this for eleven years.

She sends the memo back with tracked changes. She closes her laptop. She sits for a moment in a quiet office.

The moment passes. She opens her laptop. There are fourteen more emails.

This scene is not a tragedy. It is not even unusual. By every external measure, this lawyer is successful. She is a partner at a major firm, which means she survived a selection process with brutal attrition. She makes more than her parents earned in their lifetimes. She is, on the credential machine’s ledger, a clear success.

What the ledger does not record is the thing she felt, and didn’t quite feel, in that moment of quiet.

The Machine

The legal profession has built one of the most elaborate credentialing architectures in any professional field. Law school rankings, which sort applicants by LSAT scores and undergraduate GPA into numerical hierarchies. Law review membership, granted by a competition administered by the students who won the prior year’s competition. Federal clerkships, which confer prestige in inverse proportion to the court’s distance from Washington. Lateral market signals, which communicate your value in basis points of book of business. The partnership track, which sorts again, and then again, until the survivors emerge at the other end with the title and the equity stake, and sometimes discover, at that moment, that the thing they had been pursuing is not quite what they imagined.

Each of these credentials is real. Each one communicates something true about the person who holds it. The LSAT measures something. Law review membership demonstrates something. A federal clerkship is genuinely formative. None of this is fiction.

But what the credential machine communicates, taken as a system, is not who you are. It is how legible you are to institutions that need to sort people quickly. Those are different things, and confusing them has consequences that accumulate slowly and arrive suddenly.

The machine is not malicious. It is not a conspiracy. It is a sorting mechanism that does exactly what sorting mechanisms do: it optimizes for the characteristics it can measure, and it renders invisible the characteristics it cannot. The credential machine can measure your LSAT score. It cannot measure whether contract litigation is the problem you were built to solve. It can measure your law school GPA. It cannot measure whether the clients you serve are the ones whose problems feel, to you, like problems worth the years of your life you are spending on them.

The sorting function is useful. It is just not a compass.

What Emerson Saw

Ralph Waldo Emerson was not writing about lawyers. He was writing about poets. But what he observed in 1844, in an essay called The Poet, applies to every professional who has chosen a credential over a calling, or who has discovered, partway through a career, that they may have done so without quite realizing it.

Emerson distinguished between two kinds of people: those who are shaped by their circumstances (who become what the institution needs them to become) and those who are compelled by a vocation they did not entirely choose. The first group, he argued, becomes legible. The second group becomes present. These are not the same achievement.

His argument in Self-Reliance goes further. The person who leads with credentials, Emerson suggests, is confessing something: that they trust the institution’s judgment about their value more than their own. That they have outsourced the question of who they are to the bodies authorized to certify it. This is not vanity or weakness. It is the rational response to a system that rewards legibility and punishes ambiguity. But it is also, Emerson insists, a form of self-abandonment so gradual that most people never notice when it happened.

The résumé answers a question: What have you done that institutions will recognize?

The compass answers a different question: What are you here to do?

These two questions can, in fortunate cases, produce the same answer. But the credential machine is not designed to produce that fortune. It is designed to produce lawyers. Legible, credentialed, institutionally sorted lawyers, not this particular lawyer, with this particular nature and this particular set of problems they were built to solve. The machine does not know who you are. It knows what you scored.

But there is something the machine is missing that is prior to the score, and Emerson named it in his first book, published in 1836, eight years before The Poet, before Self-Reliance had found its famous formulations.

Nature is Emerson’s foundational claim: there is a reality prior to institutional construction. The credential machine did not create it. The rankings did not measure it. The clerkship did not confer it. What it is: the actual constitution of what a person is, their specific quality of attention, the problems that genuinely absorb them, the work that activates the deepest current of what they are. Emerson called this a person’s nature, and he meant the word literally. Not temperament. Not personality. The actual ground of what you are and what you are for, which exists beneath everything the machine has layered on top of it.

In Nature, Emerson describes a moment he called the “transparent eyeball”: the experience of genuine perception when the ego steps aside and what remains is direct contact with reality as it actually is. The machine forces the lawyer to serve the ego and the institution. The compass does the opposite: it strips the ego away so the lawyer can serve the law itself. This is closer to seeing the forest for the trees than to any flow state, closer to apprehending the whole than to any feeling of comfortable fit. The lawyer who has been there knows. Not because it was comfortable. Because it was real.

The compass, in other words, is not a preference. It is a response to something that was always there. The machine did not create it. It can only obscure it.

A compass is a crude instrument. It tells you nothing about the destination. But it was enough to let the ancient mariners leave familiar coastlines and discover the world.

The Man Who Didn’t Use the Machine

Robert Houghton Jackson was born in 1892 in Spring Creek, Pennsylvania. He attended Albany Law School for one year, then left. He apprenticed under a country lawyer named Frank Mott in Jamestown, New York, reading cases in Mott’s office, accompanying him to court, learning the practice of law by practicing it. He was admitted to the bar in 1913 without a law degree. By the credential machine’s logic, he was not quite legible.

He went on to become Solicitor General of the United States, then Attorney General, then Associate Justice of the Supreme Court. In 1945, Harry Truman appointed him Chief American Prosecutor at the Nuremberg Tribunal, where Jackson had to construct the legal architecture for trying crimes that had no precedent in international law: inventing categories, drafting charges, making arguments before a court that had never existed for conduct that had never been prosecuted.

Jackson did not accomplish this because he had the right credentials. He accomplished it because he had followed, his entire career, a compass that pointed consistently at a single question: what is the relationship between law and political power, and what does it mean for a civilization when that relationship breaks down? That question led him from a country law office in upstate New York through the New Deal to the Supreme Court to a courtroom in Bavaria where it mattered more than any credential could have prepared him for.

He was also, by his own account and by the account of everyone who knew him, an Emersonian. He had read Emerson deeply and returned to him throughout his life. He modeled his professional conduct (his insistence on independent judgment, his resistance to capture by any single client or institution, his commitment to articulating the law rather than merely winning cases) on principles he found in Emerson’s essays.

Jackson appears here not as a model to emulate. Very few lawyers will construct international tribunals. He appears here as something more useful: proof that the compass route exists. That following your actual nature rather than the credential sequence is not naive. That the lawyers history occasionally requires are not the ones most legible to the machine.

He is also a provocation. Jackson worked with what was in front of him: the cases available in Jamestown, the clients who walked through Mott’s door, the government positions that opened during the New Deal. He did not wait for the right credential to arrive before beginning. He oriented himself by compass and moved.

One more thing needs to be said about Jackson, honestly. He is not offered here as a spotless figure. His record on race (including his role in legal decisions that now stand as among the profession’s most consequential failures) belongs to the full account of who he was and what he did. The direction he followed is what matters for this argument. And that direction is not his alone.

If the compass points toward a reality prior to institutional construction (toward a nature that existed before any ranking system sorted for it), then it is available to everyone the machine has excluded as surely as it was available to Jackson. The machine has historically denied the compass route to lawyers who did not look like him: through bar exam barriers, through clerkship networks that ran on personal connection, through the entire architecture of the credentialing system that this series examines. The Emersonian argument is a critique of that denial, not a celebration of the one person who navigated around it in one historical moment. The direction is universal. The machine is what distributes access to it unequally. That is part of the indictment.

What This Series Is

The Emersonian Lawyer is a series of essays about what Ralph Waldo Emerson’s philosophy has to say to the practicing lawyer, the law student, and the legal professional who has begun to suspect that the credential machine and the compass are pointing in different directions.

It is not a wellness series. It will not offer you balance, or resilience strategies, or advice about billing fewer hours. The legal profession already has those books, and they are not wrong so much as insufficient. They treat the symptoms without touching the diagnosis.

The diagnosis, stated plainly, is this: the legal profession has built a system that produces competent lawyers who are estranged from their vocation, and it has done so systematically, at scale, for decades. The ABA’s own data (which shows sustained rates of anxiety, depression, and professional dissatisfaction that are among the highest of any credentialed profession) is not an anomaly. It is the system’s output.

Emerson has something to say about this. Not because he was writing about lawyers, but because he was writing about exactly this: what happens to a person who has built a career around institutional legibility rather than genuine vocation, and what it looks like to navigate back toward the compass.

This series has been shaped by an ongoing conversation with Professor Kevin Lee, a legal philosopher whose work in civic personalism offers both a challenge and a complement to the Emersonian framework. His challenge, which this series will engage directly: does the compass route lead somewhere genuinely universal, or has it historically been available only to lawyers who already had standing, who looked like Jackson, who moved in the networks that the machine was built to serve? That challenge sharpens the argument. The answer this series offers: the compass points toward something prior to institutional sorting, available to everyone the machine has excluded. The machine is what restricts access. Naming that restriction is part of what Emerson was doing, and part of what this series does.

This series will work through five of Emerson’s core essays (Self-Reliance, Compensation, Circles, The Poet, and The Over-Soul) and the foundational Nature, which grounds all of them. Each essay will apply one of these texts to a specific friction in legal professional life. Along the way, Jackson will serve as the series’ biographical protagonist: not a saint, not a simplified hero, but a lawyer who made the Emersonian moves in real institutional conditions and left a documented record of how he did it, with an honest accounting of where he fell short.

The essays that follow will be specific. They will name the credential machine by its parts. They will apply Emersonian philosophy to the billable hour, the lateral market, the partnership track, the in-house role, the captured judgment of the associate who softens the memo because softening memos is what the institution requires.

They will not tell you what to do. Emerson does not tell you what to do. What he does (what this series will attempt) is to give you vocabulary for something you may already sense but have not quite found the language to name.

The compass is always there. The machine is very loud. But the compass is always there.

Jeff Cox, Esq. is Director of Brand at Steno and a Florida-barred attorney with a career bridging legal operations at Citigroup, legal data and analytics at UniCourt, thought leadership strategy at Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP, and AI product marketing at vLex. A prolific writer with over 100 bylines in Above the Law, Legaltech News, ABA journals, and other leading publications, he served as 2025 Board Chair of Bay Area Legal Services, a Tampa Bay nonprofit law firm providing free civil legal services to low-income residents, and currently serves on its Development Council and AI Working Group.

The post The Emersonian Lawyer: The Compass And The Resume appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Sorry, Associates, But Those Knicks Seats Were Always Going To Clients
articles.content:
编者按：欢迎来到我们的每日专栏“每日语录”。

“这类门票是给铂金A级客户的。当律师面临这种选择时，绝大多数人会选择送给客户，因为这样做能赢得客户的好感。”

——欧文·基什纳（Irwin Kishner），赫里克·费恩斯坦律师事务所体育法与企业部门联合主席，在接受《美国律师》采访时就大型律师事务所如何处理昨晚尼克斯队比赛珍贵门票的问题发表上述看法。吉布森·邓恩律师事务所在一场为“梦想花园基金会”筹款的拍卖会上获胜，获得了场边座位。至于基什纳本人？他选择在家看比赛。

斯蒂西·扎雷夫斯基（Staci Zaretsky）是Above the Law的执行主编，自2011年起在此工作。她非常欢迎读者与她联系，如有任何线索、问题、意见或批评，欢迎发送电子邮件。你可以在Bluesky、X/Twitter和Threads上关注她，或在LinkedIn上与她联系。

这篇文章《抱歉，初级律师，那些尼克斯队座位本来就是给客户的》首次发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. note: Welcome to our daily feature, Quote of the Day.

These kinds of tickets go to platinum A-plus clients. The vast majority of people (lawyers) faced with this decision give to clients because you get such goodwill with this kind of thing.

—  Irwin Kishner, co-chair of Herrick Feinstein’s sports law and corporate department, in comments given to the American Lawyer, concerning how Biglaw firms handled coveted tickets to last night’s Knicks game. Gibson Dunn scored courtside seats after winning an auction benefiting the Garden of Dreams Foundation. As for Kishner? He watched the game from home.

Staci Zaretsky is the managing editor of Above the Law, where she’s worked since 2011. She’d love to hear from you, so please feel free to email her with any tips, questions, comments, or critiques. You can follow her on Bluesky, X/Twitter, and Threads, or connect with her on LinkedIn.

The post Sorry, Associates, But Those Knicks Seats Were Always Going To Clients appeared first on Above the Law.
